es.meaning: That is; I mean — the conversational rephraser.
es.example: Llega el viernes, o sea, pasado mañana.
es.exampleTranslation: She arrives Friday — that is, the day after tomorrow.
es.regionNote: Overuse is a class-marked stereotype in both Spain (pijo) and Mexico (fresa) — fine to use, risky to lean on. Reviewer to confirm framing.

it.meaning: That is; I mean — the conversational rephraser.
it.exampleTranslation: She arrives Friday — that is, the day after tomorrow.

bridge.note: Unguessable in either direction, and you'll use it constantly: both languages build their rephraser on 'to be' — o sea is a subjunctive of ser, cioè is ciò+è ('that is') — but nothing on the surface tells you they correspond.

interference.es_to_it: The calque 'o sia' lands on a real Italian word — ossia — but the wrong one: ossia is formal, written 'or rather'. The conversational filler is cioè.
interference.it_to_es: The calque 'eso es' means 'exactly!', not 'I mean'. The rephraser is o sea (conversational) or es decir (formal).

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. The o sea→ossia near-miss (real word, wrong register) is the subtlest trap in the bank so far.

cue: I mean… / that is to say (self-correcting mid-sentence)
